data.title: BNP Paribas SA reports Q2 results
data.description:
• BNP Paribas SA (BNPQY): Q2 Net income of €4.35B.
  • Revenue of €14.09B.
  • CET1 ratio of 13%.
  • 2026 interim dividend: €3.23 payable on 28 September 2026.

data.title: Yelp giving ChatGPT access to customer reviews, business information -- report
data.description:
Yelp (YELP) is licensing its reviews, photos, and business information to OpenAI (OPENAI), giving ChatGPT access to richer business data when generating responses, according to Axios. 

Terms of the agreement were not disclosed, and the arrangement with ChatGPT does not limit Yelp (YELP) from making similar deals with competitors. Yelp (YELP) currently provides data to Yahoo+ (APO) and Apple (AAPL) Maps. A previous arrangement with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) eventually resulted in Yelp (YELP) suing Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) for prioritizing Google results over comparable results from Yelp.

Rather than develop a competing product, Yelp (YELP) will provide the information to the chatbot in return for the Yelp branding and appropriate links to the site to be included in the AI-generated response.

Yelp’s (YELP) “Request a Quote” feature will also be included eventually, enabling users to contact the merchant directly through ChatGPT.

The partnership gave Yelp (YELP) shares a shot in the arm into Thursday’s open, but that stock has since surrendered gains and is lower for a third straight day.

data.title: Carter Bank & Trust reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Carter Bank & Trust press release (CARE): Q2 EPS of $1.31.
  • Net interest income was $40.0 million.

data.title: UBS raises United Rentals estimates after strong quarter eases margin concerns
data.description:
United Rentals (URI) drew a bullish response from UBS after posting stronger-than-expected second-quarter results, with the investment bank saying the equipment rental giant's execution and improving demand support further earnings growth.

UBS analyst Steven Fisher reiterated his Buy rating on United Rentals (URI) and raised his price target to $1,350 from its previous level of $1,300 after the company reported earnings that beat Wall Street expectations and lifted its full-year outlook. Fisher said investors had entered the quarter expecting a beat-and-raise, but lingering concerns over margins made the results more impressive.

"We think investors should be positively surprised by the margin growth and better than expected fleet productivity," Fisher wrote.

The analyst said United Rentals (URI) continues to benefit from growing activity on large non-residential construction projects while demand in local markets remains stable. He added that the company has been effective at putting more equipment to work as manufacturers meet requests for additional fleet, a trend he said should also benefit equipment suppliers.

Fleet productivity rose 3.4% during the quarter, comfortably ahead of the roughly 2.0% consensus expectation, driven in part by stronger-than-expected equipment utilization and higher rental rates, Fisher said. Rental gross margins also exceeded expectations, while a gain from the sale of scaffolding assets was recorded outside the rental business and therefore did not inflate margin performance.

For investors, the results reinforce expectations that spending on large industrial and infrastructure projects will accelerate during the second half of the year, supporting faster earnings growth for equipment rental companies.

Fisher also said margin trends could continue improving as fuel and delivery costs ease and the company completes restructuring initiatives that are expected to generate roughly $45 million to $50 million in annual savings. United Rentals (URI) reported about $12 million in restructuring benefits during the second quarter.

Following the earnings report, UBS increased its financial forecasts, raising revenue estimates for fiscal 2026 through 2028 by 2.4%, 4.0% and 5.2%, respectively. The firm also lifted its adjusted EBITDA estimates by 3.2%, 3.6% and 4.8% over the same period, citing stronger fleet utilization, moderating cost pressures and continued momentum in non-residential construction.

UBS now forecasts adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $8.11 billion in 2026, $8.87 billion in 2027 and $9.58 billion in 2028.

Fisher's new $1,350 price target is based on a 10.5-times enterprise value-to-ebitda multiple, reflecting what he views as improving growth prospects and execution, even though the valuation sits above the stock's typical trading range over the past two years. He noted the shares have traded at similar multiples during previous periods of strong investor optimism.

data.title: Pinnacle outlines 2026 revenue of $5.05B-$5.1B while targeting CET1 of 10.25%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we reported diluted EPS of $2.07 and adjusted diluted EPS of $2.50, excluding $82 million of pretax adjusted items." (President, CEO & Director Kevin Blair)
  • "Loans grew $2.9 billion linked quarter, ahead of our expectations." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "Deposits were up $795 million..." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "...earning assets, up 4% quarter-over-quarter led to 2% growth in net interest income." (President, CEO & Director Blair)
  • "Credit performance continues to be a real strength... charge-offs remain low and NPAs declined this quarter to 50 basis points." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "Preliminary CET1 increased 12 basis points this quarter... inclusive of roughly 14% annualized loan growth we experienced in Q2." (President, CEO & Director Blair)
  • "We added 74 experienced revenue producers this quarter... Momentum has carried into the third quarter with another 34 producers who have already started or accepted offers in the first half of July." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "Retention, excluding merger-related synergies, is 94% year-to-date." (President, CEO & Director Blair)
  • "Net interest income... was $956 million, up 2%... from the first quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Andrew Gregory) "Net interest margin came in at 3.44%, down 9 basis points versus the first quarter..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)
  • "We repositioned approximately $1 billion of municipal securities into more liquid investments..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory) "...we issued $750 million of senior debt..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)

Outlook

  • "Our broad guidance ranges are unchanged for 2026." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "Loan growth is tracking at the top end of our 9% to 11% range and deposits in the middle of our 8% to 10% range." (President, CEO & Director Blair)
  • "We are now expecting full year NIM in the 3.44% to 3.47% range." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "...we continue to expect to be well within our revenue outlook and trending more specifically to $5.05 billion to $5.1 billion." (President, CEO & Director Blair)
  • "We expect adjusted expenses in the middle of our $2.675 billion to $2.775 billion guidance." (President, CEO & Director Blair) "Credit remains within our 20 to 25 basis point charge-off range..." (President, CEO & Director Blair)
  • The company kept its 2026 loan, deposit, expense, tax-rate, and charge-off ranges consistent with Q1, while tightening its full-year NIM expectation to "3.44% to 3.47%" (President, CEO & Director Blair) versus Q1’s "approximately 3.5%" (President, CEO & Director Blair).

Financial results

  • "Adjusted diluted EPS increased 5% versus the prior quarter and 25% versus second quarter 2025 results." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory) "...year-to-date adjusted return on average tangible common equity of 17.7%." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)
  • "Period-end loans increased $2.9 billion or 14% annualized from the first quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory) "Excluding the decline in public funds, core deposits grew $963 million..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)
  • "Adjusted non-interest revenue declined $12 million from the first quarter, driven largely by lower BHG income." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory) "Income from our equity method investment in BHG totaled $24 million in the second quarter..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)
  • "We incurred $51 million of nonrecurring merger expenses during the quarter..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory) "Net charge-offs were $48 million or 22 basis points..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory) "Our preliminary common equity Tier 1 ratio ended the quarter at 9.93%..." (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)

Q&A

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked why margin performance differed from the mid-quarter update; Executive VP & CFO Gregory: "The change... is really on the asset side" citing "decline in SOFR rates" and "PAA coming in a little lighter than expected," adding, "we think that that's going to be a little tailwind to the third quarter."
  • John McDonald, Truist: probed deposit mix and competition; Executive VP & CFO Gregory: "we believe the seasonal impacts will contribute $1.5 billion to $2 billion to growth in the second half of the year" and said broker deposits should rise; President, CEO & Director Blair: "we don't compete on price" and referenced a "3.72 spread" between new loan and new deposit rates.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: pressed on longer-run NIM and Cat IV liquidity impacts; Executive VP & CFO Gregory: "you're right to think about the liquidity impacts in 2027 being a further headwind" and quantified, "$1 billion of long-term debt is about 1 to 1.5 basis points margin per billion."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: revisited BHG strategy/strategic actions; Executive VP & CFO Gregory: "We raised our revenue guide on the investment side for 2026" and "There is no update to give on their strategic options."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: asked about upside to loan-growth guidance and the loan-to-deposit ratio; President, CEO & Director Blair: "Yes, there's upside" but noted utilization added roughly "$0.5 billion of growth" and is not forecast; Executive VP & CFO Gregory: "Loan-to-deposit ratio is not a metric that we really manage to..."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: asked about cash build and expense trajectory; Executive VP & CFO Gregory: "I would probably give a range of $4 billion to $4.5 billion for cash balances in the second half of the year" and said synergy progress supports "our 40% target for this year" and "75% next year."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral on funding/margin durability, with repeated framing around longer-run NIM and liquidity build, including, "My assumption is we are headed lower somewhere into the 3.30s" (Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and often rebuttal-oriented on competition/funding, including, "we don't compete on price" (President, CEO & Director Blair) and "we're not seeing that spread tightening that we're hearing from some of the others" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory).
  • Versus Q1’s “hit the ground running” messaging, Q2 leaned more into proving merger execution with added specificity, including, "We are 6 months in and the results are doing the talking." (President, CEO & Director Blair)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 emphasized stronger linked-quarter growth than Q1 discussed (Q2: "Loans grew $2.9 billion" (President, CEO & Director Blair); Q1: "over $2 billion in loan growth" (President, CEO & Director Blair)) while maintaining the same full-year loan and deposit growth ranges.
  • Margin messaging shifted from Q1’s "approximately 3.5%" full-year framing (President, CEO & Director Blair) to Q2’s narrower "3.44% to 3.47%" expectation (President, CEO & Director Blair), with management attributing pressure more to Cat IV liquidity actions than to competitive pricing.
  • Merger integration discussion moved from Q1’s large one-time cost focus ("$275 million of merger-related expenses" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)) to Q2’s lower run-rate integration spend ("$51 million of nonrecurring merger expenses" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)) and explicit synergy pacing ("40%... this year" and "75% next year" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory)).

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged margin headwinds tied to Cat IV liquidity and funding actions, including "debt issuance" and "growing cash and securities to assets" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory), while pointing to offsets such as "core deposit growth to outpace loan growth" in 2H (Executive VP & CFO Gregory).
  • BHG earnings variability remained a focus after a quarterly decline: "Adjusted non-interest revenue declined... driven largely by lower BHG income" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory), though management said a paused distribution channel "have since resumed" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory).
  • Capital build timing drew questions around the "10.25% target" (Executive VP & CFO Gregory), with management noting growth-driven RWA consumption can slow near-term accretion.

Final takeaway

Management described Q2 as another merger “proof point,” highlighting $2.50 adjusted EPS, strong linked-quarter loan and deposit growth, and improving credit metrics, while keeping 2026 guidance ranges intact. The key change in messaging versus Q1 was a more defined full-year NIM expectation of 3.44% to 3.47%, with executives repeatedly tying near-term margin pressure to Category IV liquidity positioning (cash/securities build and debt issuance) rather than to price competition, and reiterating that they expect continued capital accretion toward a 10.25% CET1 target alongside ongoing hiring-driven growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Comcast outlines separation timeline of approximately 1 year while expecting modest improvements starting in the third quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Comcast (CMCSA) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “Before Mike and Jason take you through the quarter, I'd like to spend a few minutes on the separation we announced 3 weeks ago.” (Chief Executive Officer, President, Director and Director of Comcast Cable Communications Inc Brian Roberts)

  • “There's real excitement about taking 2 exceptional companies and giving each the focus and agility to win in markets that are changing fast.” (Chief Executive Officer, President, Director and Director of Comcast Cable Communications Inc Brian Roberts)

  • “That's also why this is the right time for Michael Angelakis to return.” (Chief Executive Officer, President, Director and Director of Comcast Cable Communications Inc Brian Roberts)

  • “In the second quarter, we crossed 10 million lines for the first time... just 7% penetration of the total addressable lines in our footprint.” (Chief Executive Officer, President, Director and Director of Comcast Cable Communications Inc Brian Roberts)

  • “In just 6 years, we've built Peacock into a streaming business with real scale in the U.S.... reached profitability.” (Chief Executive Officer, President, Director and Director of Comcast Cable Communications Inc Brian Roberts)

  • “We are off and running... with the goal of completing the separation in approximately 1 year.” (Co-CEO & Director Michael Cavanagh)

  • “A key part of our work is on the balance sheet and capital structure as our intention is to set both companies up with strong investment-grade profiles.” (Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh)

  • “In the second quarter, revenue increased 5%... Adjusted EBITDA declined 5%.” (Chief Financial Officer Jason Armstrong)

Outlook

  • “We are operating under the assumption that the market will remain intensely competitive.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “We expect modest improvements starting in the third quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “Early free line conversion cohorts are tracking in line with our expectations, and we continue to expect a significant majority of these customers to convert to paid relationships as roll-offs accelerate in the second half of the year.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “Attendance across the broader Orlando market began to soften in June, and that trend has continued into the third quarter.” (Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh)

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided, but the “fiscalQuarter” format did not match the required Q1–Q4 format, so no transcript-to-estimates comparisons are included.

Financial results

  • “Adjusted earnings per share were $1.04, and we generated $4.6 billion of free cash flow in the quarter, of which we returned $2.1 billion to shareholders, including $900 million in share repurchases.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “Broadband subscriber losses improved by 34,000 year-over-year to a loss of 167,000.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “Broadband ARPU declined 3.8% in the quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “We added 448,000 net lines... We ended the quarter with 10.2 million total lines.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “At Theme Parks, revenue increased 3%, while EBITDA declined 5%.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “Peacock reached profitability for the first time, generating $189 million of EBITDA in the quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

Q&A

  • John Hodulik, UBS: “Can you discuss the competitive dynamics in the broadband market?” Steven Croney, Chief Executive Officer of Connectivity & Platforms: “Competition remains intense... some of the behavior has been irrational.”

  • Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson: “There is a robust debate as to whether Starlink broadband is purely a rural solution...” and Vikash, New Street: “Starlink exploring potential partnerships...” Chief Financial Officer Armstrong: “We currently are not seeing them as a meaningful competitive factor in our markets... we actually already do work with Starlink.”

  • Peter Supino, Wolfe Research: “Can you talk about your mobile strategy... converting paid lines and driving premium uptake...” Chief Executive Officer Croney: “Our premium mobile adoption continues to strengthen, and we're now exceeding 30% sell-in.”

  • Steven Cahall, Wells Fargo: “Is this still the outlook?” (ARPU/EBITDA pressure abating) Chief Financial Officer Armstrong: “We expect modest improvements starting in the third quarter.”

  • Jessica Reif Ehrlich, Bank of America: “Do you believe NBCU has sufficient scale to compete and grow on its own?” Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh: “I do believe that NBCUniversal and Sky do have the heft... to continue to be a major player as an independent.”

  • Mike Ng, Goldman Sachs / Kutgun Maral, Evercore / John Hodulik, UBS: Parks softness and drivers. Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh: “The softness... is essentially weakness in attendance... driven by some weakness in consumer sentiment and higher travel costs.”

  • Kutgun Maral, Evercore: “Does that represent a durable profitability inflection?” Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh: “We do expect it to continue to improve on an annual basis, but profitability is going to vary quarter-by-quarter.”

  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: “Provide more detail on target leverage and dividend policy...” Chief Financial Officer Armstrong: “Nothing to add relative to what we said 3 weeks ago upon announcement.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing, concentrating on competitive threats and capital structure, including: “some of the behavior has been irrational” (Chief Executive Officer Croney) and “provide more detail on target leverage and dividend policy” (Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive/contained on capital return details in Q&A, including: “the reaction has been overwhelmingly positive” (Chief Executive Officer, President, Director and Director of Comcast Cable Communications Inc Roberts) and “Nothing to add” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong).

  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on early traction, this quarter’s tone added more caution on near-term parks and the competitive set, while reiterating improvement timing, including: “We expect modest improvements starting in the third quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call introduced the separation as the central strategic shift, including: “the goal of completing the separation in approximately 1 year.” (Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh)

  • Connectivity commentary moved from Q1’s “incremental pressure... in the second quarter” to Q2’s “expect modest improvements starting in the third quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • Parks discussion shifted from Q1’s strength around Epic to Q2’s acknowledgment of softness, including: “attendance... began to soften in June.” (Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh)

  • Peacock discussion shifted from Q1 “approach profitability” to Q2 profitability achieved, including: “Peacock reached profitability for the first time.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

Risks and concerns

  • “The broadband market remains highly competitive... fixed wireless remains aggressive, satellite is emerging as another alternative and convergence-based promotional activity remains elevated.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

  • “The operating environment has softened more than we anticipated.” (Co-CEO & Director Cavanagh)

  • “We issued updated pro forma trending schedules to reflect the removal of Sky Germany... following the sale of that business on May 31.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 around the pending separation, with an “approximately 1 year” execution target, while describing a near-term investment phase in Connectivity & Platforms that pressured ARPU and EBITDA but is expected to show “modest improvements starting in the third quarter.” In Content & Experiences, management highlighted Peacock’s first profitable quarter and strong Studios performance, while flagging a softer parks demand trend emerging in June and continuing into Q3, and reiterated that capital return details remain unchanged beyond the prior separation announcement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs projects $575M Q3 adjusted EBITDA while targeting sub 2.5x leverage by next year
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "After several quarters of talking about the future earnings power of this company, we can finally point to tangible evidence that the progression we have been forecasting is now reality," said Chairman, President & CEO Lourenco Goncalves. "During the second quarter, we returned to positive free cash flow and tripled our adjusted EBITDA from the first quarter."
  • "That will be more visible in Q3, in which we are expecting to more than double our Q2 EBITDA," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves, adding, "With our third quarter adjusted EBITDA guidance of $575 million, we have a situation where higher prices, lower costs and higher shipping volumes will all be converging at once."
  • "One important item to mention today, we have officially kicked off negotiations with the United Steel Workers Union to renew our collective bargain agreement," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves. "And I'm pleased to say that the process is off to a constructive and productive start."
  • "Before turning it over, I would like to recognize Celso's appointment to our Board of Directors as President and CFO that was announced this morning," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves. "It also marks the early stages of a transition in leadership. I'm not going anywhere anytime soon."
  • "Our adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter was $286 million, our best quarter in 2 years," said Executive VP & CFO Celso Goncalves. "Second quarter shipments were just over 4 million tons, down sequentially from the previous quarter due to the maintenance outages we underwent during the quarter."
  • "Our average selling price increased by $76 per ton," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "Based on this, we expect our average price to increase another $55 per ton in Q3."

Outlook

  • "We expect adjusted EBITDA of approximately $575 million in the third quarter, which would be our strongest quarter in 3 years," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "With where the curve for HRC stands today, we would expect even further improvement on that figure in the fourth quarter, even with the typical seasonal slowdown we usually see around the holidays."
  • "We'll have an opportunity in the coming months to reset a large portion of our fixed price contracts substantially higher," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "[W]e estimate [this] will represent a $500 million EBITDA improvement year-over-year."
  • Compared with last quarter’s framing that Q2 would be the best quarter in nearly two years and Q3 would show the earnings power more fully, management’s guidance language shifted to explicit quarterly targets and a stronger conviction statement. "Cleveland-Cliffs is entering the strongest earnings environment that we have seen in years," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves.

Financial results

  • "Our adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter was $286 million," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves, and "after 2 years of negative free cash flow, we finally flipped back to positive in the second quarter."
  • "Second quarter shipments were just over 4 million tons," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves, and "we expect to see steel shipment volumes above 4.3 million tons in the third quarter as the order book remains strong and backlogs are extended."
  • "As for unit costs, as previously guided, the inventory lag effect from last quarter and our maintenance outages pushed costs up quarter-over-quarter," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "But with that behind us, we should see a $10 per ton reduction in costs into Q3."
  • "[W]e are now under contract on all of our major property sales with earnest money in our control in all cases," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves. "The bulk of the $400 million proceeds from our property sales are expected to come in the second half of this year."

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: asked about timing and scope of resetting non-auto fixed-price contracts and expectations for auto contract resets; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves responded, "it's a process that starts in earnest in the second half of the of this year," and on auto pricing, "we are going to reset the numbers higher."
  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: asked about Q4 cost direction; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves answered, "Yes, we expect further improvements," citing "more optimized schedules at the mills, thanks to our work with Palantir."
  • Samuel McKinney, KeyBanc: asked how much of the Q3 shipment uplift is automotive-driven; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves said, "I would say half." McKinney also pressed on payables’ impact on free cash flow; Executive VP & CFO Goncalves said, "The payables were largely driven by things like raw materials going up, additional maintenance work and things like that."
  • Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs: asked for Stelco pricing color and the $500 million uplift bridge; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves said, "the pricing gap has closed," but "we haven't seen yet the same type of impact with the galvanized steel." He added, "if I need to make any changes in the Canadian footprint, it will be all affecting galvanized and producing more hot rolled."
  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: asked about capacity restarts and Dearborn; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves said restarting hinges on customers’ commitment, adding, "I need the conviction" and "we're not going to go until they are ready to go." Giles also asked about debt paydown; Executive VP & CFO Goncalves said, "debt paydown is going to be our #1 capital allocation priority," and "we don't have anything maturing until 2029."
  • Richard Garchitorena, Barclays: asked what drives the expectation that Q4 outperforms Q3 despite seasonality; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves emphasized contracting visibility, saying, "we have a good visibility into volumes" and "we already baked in" holiday shipment reductions.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, concentrating on contract reset mechanics, free-cash-flow quality, Canada/Stelco profitability, and the durability of Q4 improvement; questions repeatedly pressed for bridges and timing (e.g., "what's driving the incremental improvement" from Barclays).
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and remained forceful in Q&A, with repeated conviction language such as "we have a good visibility" and "we are going to reset the numbers higher," while also signaling discipline on transactions: "we're not desperate to do anything," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves.
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on one-timers and normalization, management’s tone shifted toward higher certainty and explicit targets, including "we are expecting to more than double our Q2 EBITDA" and "we expect adjusted EBITDA of approximately $575 million in the third quarter."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance posture tightened from Q1’s directional setup ("Q2 should be our best quarter in nearly 2 years" and "Q3 should be much better than Q2," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves last quarter) to Q2’s explicit quarterly adjusted EBITDA guide for Q3 and stated expectation for Q4 improvement.
  • Strategic emphasis expanded from Q1’s operational normalization, outages, and AI-partner announcement timing to Q2’s broader stance on valuation and deal pressure, with Executive VP & CFO Goncalves saying counterparties assumed Cliffs "was under pressure to transact" and that offers "have fallen short of our value threshold."
  • Analyst focus stayed consistent on contracts, costs, and cash generation, but Q2 added more probing on Stelco’s pricing mix and the conditions for restarting capacity.

Risks and concerns

  • Canada exposure remained a named risk: "Without further measures to protect fair trade in Canada, the future competitiveness of our galvanizing lines in Hamilton is at risk," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves.
  • Transaction uncertainty was framed as optionality and discipline: "discussions still remain friendly and ongoing" with POSCO, but "valuation and structure are important," said Executive VP & CFO Goncalves.
  • Labor negotiations were highlighted as underway, with management stating, "we are confident that we can reach an agreement," said Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves.

Final takeaway

Management described a shift from recovery setup to visible acceleration, anchored by Q2’s $286 million adjusted EBITDA, a return to positive free cash flow, and Q3 adjusted EBITDA guidance of approximately $575 million. Executives tied the step-up to pricing realization, higher volumes, and cost reductions, while emphasizing balance-sheet deleveraging as the top priority and signaling transaction discipline on potential asset or strategic deals, alongside ongoing labor negotiations and continued scrutiny of Canadian trade protections affecting Stelco’s finishing operations.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Teck Resources rises as Q2 profit triples on record copper prices
data.description:
Teck Resources (TECK) up 3.7% in Thursday's trading after reporting Q2 profit tripled from a year ago on the back of higher copper prices, while reaffirming full-year production guidance.

Q2 adjusted EBITDA surged to C$2.19B (~US$1.6B) from C$722M in the year-earlier quarter, while revenue rose 78% Y/Y to C$3.61B, as benchmark three-month copper prices on the London Metals Exchange jumped 41% Y/Y, powered by concerns over tight supplies and strong demand in China.

Teck (TECK) said its realized copper prices averaged $6.05/lb in Q2, up from $4.32/lb in the prior-year period, and production climbed 24.6% to 135.9K tons.

Copper production at the Quebrada Blanca mine in Chile totaled 55.8K tons, slightly higher than both Q1 and the year-ago quarter, the company said, maintaining full-year output guidance for the mine at 200K-235K tons.

Overall, the company maintained full-year guidance for copper production of 455K-530K metric tons and zinc production of 410K-460K tons, which would deliver refined zinc of 190K-230K tons.

"Operationally, we continue to increase momentum across the business," Teck (TECK) President and CEO Jonathan Price said on the company's earnings conference call, noting that Quebrada Blanca achieved a third consecutive quarter of stable operating performance.

Teck (TECK), which is in the process of merging ​with Anglo American (AAUKF) (NGLOY), said the merger approval process with Chinese regulators ​is progressing normally and that it has not received any requests for remedies ​from authorities in China.

Teck (TECK) also said it is continuing to evaluate a broad range of ‌potential feedstock ⁠sources for germanium processing as it works to expand its capabilities in the mineral; the company is North America's largest germanium producer.

data.title: Mullen Group GAAP EPS of $0.41, revenue of $609.3M
data.description:
• Mullen Group press release (MLLGF): Q2 adjusted EPS of $0.41.
  • Revenue of $609.3M (+12.6% Y/Y).
  • Operating income before depreciation and amortization reached $103.90M, up 35.60% year-over-year.
  • Net income increased 40.60% year-over-year to reach $36.00M.
